claims.excerpt: FLAP-D vacancy hits record low of 6.3%, pipeline is 83% pre-let. Capacity is being absorbed faster than it can be replaced.

tensions.text: Arizton's forecast that Germany's DC 'Power Capacity' will only 'Exceed 524 MW by 2031' is nearly an order of magnitude below both BMWK's cross-check figure (>2,700 MW installed, already reached by March 2025) and Statista's colo-only figure (1,737 MW) used as the node's primary input — suggesting Arizton is measuring a narrower or different capacity metric, which if conflated could badly distort the DE ratio.

GTAI projects Germany will reach only 2,330 MW of domestic data center power capacity 'by 2030', a lower figure than BMWK's claim that Germany already had 'an installed IT power demand of over 2,700 MW' as of March 2025 — the two government-adjacent/industry sources disagree on whether current or even 2030 capacity exceeds ~2,300-2,700 MW, undermining confidence in the 0.42 low-end cross-check derived from the BMWK figure.

Frankfurt capacity estimates diverge sharply within the same search set: JLL and neospaces cite Frankfurt 'IT Load: 745 MW' (live), while Mordor Intelligence estimates the Frankfurt market at '1.30 thousand MW' to '1.39 thousand megawatt' in 2025 — nearly double — implying the same colo-vs-total-installed scope ambiguity the node itself flags as unresolved (promotionPath: 'Reconcile colo vs total-installed scopes').

Facility counts for Germany diverge by roughly 4x: BMWK/Impact Report cite 'over 2,000 data centres' / '1,994 data centers', while Datacenterknowledge states Germany has 'nearly 500 facilities listed' — indicating no stable base-rate unit of measurement (facility count) exists for cross-validating the MW-based capacity apportionment used in the node.

CleanBridge's total IT capacity figure for Germany (1,955.0 MW) and the German Data Center Impact Report's figure (also 1,955 MW IT power, but explicitly including only 309 colocation DCs among 1,994 total DCs) sit close to Statista's 1,737 MW colo-only figure used in the node, but it is unclear whether 1,955 MW represents colo-only or total (colo+enterprise) capacity — the ambiguity the node's derivation already acknowledges.

absences: No source in this search set independently corroborates the CE-7 total colocation capacity denominator (3,451 MW) used in the node's top-down apportionment — all cross-referenced reports discuss Germany in isolation, not the CE-7 basket (DE, NL, CH, PL, CZ, AT).

No report explicitly breaks down what share of Germany's >2,700 MW 'installed IT power demand' (BMWK) is colocation versus enterprise/hyperscale self-built capacity — exactly the reconciliation the node's promotionPath calls for, and it remains unfound.

No time series of Germany's DC capacity growth (e.g., year-over-year MW additions 2022-2025) was found to validate that the 2025 snapshot values (1,737 MW colo, >2,700 MW installed) are representative rather than a point-in-time anomaly amid rapid AI-driven buildout.

No vendor-revenue-based market-share split for Germany (the reconciliation method the node's promotionPath specifies) appeared in any of the retrieved sources; all found figures are MW-capacity or facility-count based, not revenue-based.

No granular breakdown of secondary German markets (Munich, Hamburg, Dusseldorf) with MW figures was found, despite multiple sources noting their emerging growth — leaving Frankfurt/Berlin as the only quantified sub-national data points.
